indicator_text: Registration: Count of registered lobby firms or lobbyists (often used to measure the density of lobbying populations or as a proxy for activity)
section_or_category: Table 3: Indicators used to analyse lobbying activity
scoring_rule: Not specified; descriptive count. Used as direct measure (population of lobbyists) or as a proxy for activity.
source_quote: Registration | Count of registered lobby firms or lobbyists (often used to measure the density of lobbying populations or as a proxy for activity) (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:361)
notes: Most common indicator in the corpus: n=67 of 165 studies (line 376–378). Earliest indicator measured (only form before 2004; line 386–388). Inductively developed from screening the included studies, not pre-specified.

indicator_text: Expenditure: Amount of money spent on lobbying (e.g., firm or client expenditure, distinct from political contributions)
section_or_category: Table 3: Indicators used to analyse lobbying activity
scoring_rule: Not specified; descriptive monetary amount. Used as direct measure or proxy for activity.
source_quote: Expenditure | Amount of money spent on lobbying (e.g., firm or client expenditure, distinct from political contributions) (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:362)
notes: Second-most common indicator: n=56 of 165 studies (line 378). Most expenditure studies (48 of 56) use Open Secrets, biasing the literature toward US Congress (lines 414–417). Note explicit distinction from political contributions.

indicator_text: Meetings: Face-to-face meetings with public servants (e.g., elected officials, staff, bureaucrats); requests for meetings; reports of government branch(es), agency(s) or department(s) contacted; informal meetings
section_or_category: Table 3: Indicators used to analyse lobbying activity
scoring_rule: Not specified; descriptive count. Authors call meetings "the 'gold standard' of political access" (line 199–200) — phase-2 deep-dive focus.
source_quote: Meetings | Face-to-face meetings with public servants (e.g., elected officials, staff, bureaucrats); requests for meetings; reports of government branch(es), agency(s) or department(s) contacted; informal meetings (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:363–364)
notes: n=15 of 165 studies (line 379). Selected for stage-2 deep-dive analysis (lines 376–382). Compound indicator: bundles face-to-face, meeting requests, agency contact reports, and informal meetings as one category.

indicator_text: Comments: Text of written letters, submissions, comments, responses to consultations, etc.
section_or_category: Table 3: Indicators used to analyse lobbying activity
scoring_rule: Not specified; document/text-based content for qualitative or text-mining analysis.
source_quote: Comments | Text of written letters, submissions, comments, responses to consultations, etc (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:365)
notes: n=14 of 165 studies (line 379). Compound indicator: subsumes letters, submissions, consultation responses; includes both formal regulatory comment and policy-submission text.

indicator_text: Bills: Number of bills lobbied; number of groups that lobby a bill
section_or_category: Table 3: Indicators used to analyse lobbying activity
scoring_rule: Not specified; descriptive count. Two distinct counts subsumed (bills-per-actor; actors-per-bill).
source_quote: Bills | Number of bills lobbied; number of groups that lobby a bill (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:366)
notes: n=9 of 165 studies (line 379–380). Compound: combines actor-centric (how many bills a lobbyist touched) with bill-centric (how many actors lobbied a given bill).

indicator_text: Committees: Participation in committee hearings/consultations; committee membership; Congressional testimony
section_or_category: Table 3: Indicators used to analyse lobbying activity
scoring_rule: Not specified; descriptive count of participation events.
source_quote: Committees | Participation in committee hearings/consultations; committee membership; Congressional testimony (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:367)
notes: n=4 of 165 studies (line 380) — least-used indicator in the corpus. Compound: subsumes hearings, formal membership, and testimony.

indicator_text: Business — also Company, Private firm, Corporate bodies, For-profit organisations

notes: Table 6 maps the most common actor-classification scheme observed across the 15 meeting studies. Not the paper's own scheme — a synthesis of vocabulary used in the literature. Each row pairs a canonical category with its synonyms.

indicator_text: Government data — datasets produced and published by national, state, or supranational government bodies (lobbyist registers, transparency registers, finance commissions, etc.)
section_or_category: Table 4: Data sources to analyse lobbying
scoring_rule: Not specified; classification of data sources by provenance and accessibility.
source_quote: Table 4 list the datasets identified in phase 1, which we classified as government data, publicly available data and commercially available data. (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:415–417)
notes: Table 4 column 1. Includes US federal (FEC, LDA, FARA, IRS Form 990, SEC), US states (CA, MN, FL, WI, NC), EU (Transparency Register, EUR-Lex, EC calendars), and country-level registers (Ireland, Germany, France, Chile, UK, Canada, Australia). Heavy US/EU bias.

indicator_text: Publicly available data — datasets compiled by NGOs, academic projects, or transparency initiatives, available free of charge
section_or_category: Table 4: Data sources to analyse lobbying
scoring_rule: We have reviewed the websites of all non-government data sources to establish whether they were available free of charge. Where unsure, we have listed them as commercially available. (line 419–422)
source_quote: government data, publicly available data and commercially available data (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:417)
notes: Table 4 column 2. Includes Comparative Agendas Project, Open Secrets (now merged with NIMP), LobbyView, Sunlight Foundation, lobbyfacts.eu, FollowTheMoney, FOI repositories. Boundary with "commercially available" determined by free-access verification.

indicator_text: Commercially available data — paid databases (often accessible via institutional subscriptions) including business databases, lobbyist directories, and proprietary classifications
section_or_category: Table 4: Data sources to analyse lobbying
source_quote: government data, publicly available data and commercially available data (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:417)
notes: Table 4 column 3. Includes BoardEx, Compustat, Orbis, LexisNexis, Refinitiv Datastream, Thomson Reuters Business Classification, Washington Representatives. Caveat: "researchers often have free access to these sources through their university libraries. However, cost would present a significant barrier for other organisations" (lines 423–426).

indicator_text: Inclusion criterion: published in English
section_or_category: Table 2: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
scoring_rule: Not published in English, or no English full text available excluded (line 338).
source_quote: Published in English | Not published in English, or no English full text available (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:338)
notes: Table 2 row 1. Defines review boundary, not lobbying-disclosure quality.

indicator_text: Inclusion criterion: conducted original/primary research
section_or_category: Table 2: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
scoring_rule: Commentary, editorial, policy submission, book report, annual report, political party platform or news article; presents model with no empirical data; referred to other studies without conducting original research excluded (lines 339–341).
source_quote: Conducted original/primary research (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:339)
notes: Table 2 row 2.

indicator_text: Inclusion criterion: focused on the practice of lobbying or the quantity of lobby firms and lobbyists. Includes studies that (1) measured a lobbying activity (meetings, submissions, committee participation), (2) measured lobbying expenses, or (3) measured the population of lobby firms/lobbyists (registration counts).
section_or_category: Table 2: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
scoring_rule: Excluded: studies analysing only mechanisms to address lobbying (e.g., disclosure requirements); only the influence of lobbying (not the practice); only public-targeted strategies; only media framing; only revolving door; lobbying as minor component (lines 342–349).
source_quote: Focused on the practice of lobbying or the quantity of lobby firms and lobbyists. This included studies that: (1) Measured a lobbying activity undertaken to influence public servants (e.g., meetings, submissions, committee participation) (2) Measured lobbying expenses (3) Measured the population of lobby firms/lobbyists (e.g., registration counts) (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:342–348)
notes: Table 2 row 3. Critically: studies of disclosure regimes (the focus of the FOCAL 2024 corpus) were *excluded* — confirming the 2023 paper's scope is downstream-of-disclosure (what researchers can do with disclosed data) rather than upstream (how disclosure regimes are structured).

indicator_text: Inclusion criterion: used publicly available and replicable data (including FOIs subsequently shared in public repositories)
section_or_category: Table 2: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
scoring_rule: Data sources not public or easily replicable (e.g., investigative journalism, interviews, surveys, participant observation) excluded (lines 350–351).
source_quote: Used publicly available and replicable data (including FOIs subsequently shared in public repositories) (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:350–351)
notes: Table 2 row 4. Self-imposed limitation; paper acknowledges in conclusion that this excludes investigative journalism, interviews, ethnography (lines 1078–1083).

indicator_text: Inclusion criterion: methods provide reasonable detail regarding data sources and steps taken to access, clean, and analyse the data
section_or_category: Table 2: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
scoring_rule: Methods unexplained, or not described in reasonable detail to enable replication (e.g., 'data analysed by author') excluded (lines 352–353).
source_quote: Methods provide reasonable detail regarding data sources and steps taken to access, clean and analyse the data (papers/text/Lacy-Nichols_2023__aiding_empirical_research_cdoh.txt:352–353)
notes: Table 2 row 5. Filter that disproportionately excluded grey literature: 45 grey reports excluded for methods not described (line 284); only 16 grey reports passed phase 1, only 3 passed phase 2.
